label: Ubeidiya (~1.5M BCE)
sub: Earliest hominid site outside Africa; Acheulean tools and Homo erectus fossils in Jordan Valley sediments.

label: Gesher Benot Ya'aqov (~790k BCE)
sub: Earliest evidence of controlled fire use in the world; lakeside Acheulean site on the Jordan River.

label: Qesem Cave (~400–200k BCE)
sub: Archaic Homo sapiens / Homo heidelbergensis; evidence of systematic fire use and butchery. Discovered 2000.

label: Mount Carmel Caves (Tabun/Skhul/Kebara)
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. 300,000 years of habitation; Neanderthal and modern human remains from 120,000–40,000 BCE.

label: Ohalo II (~21,000 BCE)
sub: Submerged Late Paleolithic campsite; six brush huts and wild cereal remains — evidence of grain use 10,000 years before agriculture.

label: Tel Megiddo (Armageddon)
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. 26 excavated strata; key Bronze/Iron Age city guarding the Jezreel Valley pass. Site of multiple ancient battles.

label: Tel Hazor
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. Largest Bronze Age city in Canaan (~20,000 inhabitants); destroyed by Israelites per Joshua 11; excavated by Yadin 1955–58.

label: Tel Lachish
sub: Second largest Iron Age city of Judah; Lachish Reliefs (British Museum) show Assyrian siege; Lachish Ostraca document last days before Babylonian conquest.

label: Tel Beersheba
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. Iron Age Israelite city associated with the patriarchs; horned altar found intact. Biblical southern boundary 'from Dan to Beersheba'.

label: Gezer
sub: Key Canaanite/Israelite city; Gezer Calendar (oldest Hebrew inscription) found here. Excavated by Macalister 1902–09; Egyptian Pharaoh gave Gezer to Solomon as dowry.

label: Jericho (Tell es-Sultan)
sub: World's oldest continuously inhabited city (~9,500 BCE). Pre-Pottery Neolithic tower and wall; PPNA/PPNB phases excavated by Kenyon 1952–58.

label: Samaria / Sebastos
sub: Capital of the Northern Kingdom of Israel (founded ~880 BCE by Omri); rebuilt as Hellenistic/Roman Sebastos; Herod built major colonnaded city here.

label: Tel Dan
sub: Northernmost Israelite city. Tel Dan Stele (9th cent. BCE) mentioning 'House of David' — earliest extrabiblical reference to King David's dynasty.

label: Caesarea Maritima
sub: Herod's showcase Roman harbour city (~20 BCE); largest port in the eastern Mediterranean; seat of Roman governors including Pontius Pilate (Pilate Inscription found here).

label: Beit She'an (Scythopolis)
sub: Largest ancient city in the Jordan Valley; Egyptian temple from Ramesses II era; later Hellenistic Scythopolis, one of the Decapolis cities; exceptionally preserved Roman cardo.

label: Ashkelon
sub: One of five Philistine cities (Pentapolis); major Bronze Age port; continuous occupation from Canaanite through Byzantine periods; infant cemetery excavated by Harvard.

label: Shechem (Tell Balata)
sub: First capital of the Northern Kingdom of Israel. Patriarchal covenant site in Genesis; also sacred to Samaritans who still perform Passover sacrifice on nearby Mount Gerizim.

label: Jerusalem Old City
sub: UNESCO World Heritage (Jordanian nomination). Inhabited since ~3,500 BCE. Contains Temple Mount/Haram al-Sharif, Church of Holy Sepulchre, Jewish Quarter, Armenian Quarter.

label: Akko (Acre)
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. Crusader-era underground city; Crusader capital after fall of Jerusalem (1187–1291); Napoleon failed to take it in 1799; last Crusader stronghold until 1291.

label: Gaza (Ancient)
sub: One of five Philistine cities; major Bronze Age trading hub on the Via Maris; besieged by Alexander the Great (332 BCE); site of extensive but endangered archaeology.

label: Temple Mount / Haram al-Sharif
sub: Holiest site in Judaism (traditional location of First and Second Temples); third holiest site in Islam (Dome of the Rock, Al-Aqsa Mosque). Administered by Jordanian Waqf.

label: Church of Holy Sepulchre
sub: Venerated by Christians as the site of Jesus' crucifixion, burial, and resurrection. Built by Constantine (326 CE); current structure largely Crusader (12th cent.). Shared by six Christian denominations.

label: Qumran (Dead Sea Scrolls)
sub: Sectarian Jewish community (~2nd century BCE–68 CE); 11 caves yielded ~981 Dead Sea Scrolls manuscripts — the oldest known Hebrew Bible texts, predating previous copies by 1,000 years.

label: Church of the Nativity, Bethlehem
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. Oldest continuously operating Christian church in the world, built by Constantine (~330 CE). Venerated as the birthplace of Jesus; located in Palestinian Authority territory.

label: Mount Tabor
sub: Traditional site of the Transfiguration of Jesus; site of Barak's victory over Sisera (Judges 4–5); Crusader-era and modern Franciscan and Greek Orthodox basilicas on summit.

label: Masada
sub: UNESCO World Heritage. Herodian fortress on Dead Sea cliff; last Jewish holdout in 73 CE Great Revolt. Site of mass suicide per Josephus. Excavated by Yadin 1963–65.

label: Battle of Hattin (1187 CE)
sub: Decisive battle where Saladin annihilated the Crusader army on July 4, 1187, capturing King Guy and the True Cross; led directly to the fall of Jerusalem.

label: Cave of Letters (Bar Kokhba Cache)
sub: Nahal Hever cave yielding Bar Kokhba's personal letters (132–135 CE), a Roman bronze archive, and personal belongings of Jewish refugees — vivid window into the last Jewish revolt against Rome.

label: Tel Aviv
sub: Founded 1909 as first modern Hebrew city; today centre of Israel's tech economy ('Silicon Wadi'); UNESCO World Heritage for Bauhaus 'White City' architecture (4,000 Bauhaus buildings).

label: Knesset (Israeli Parliament)
sub: Israel's 120-seat unicameral parliament, inaugurated in its current building in 1966 in Jerusalem. Venue of Sadat's historic speech (1977) and Camp David ratifications.
